title: Document public ReplicationApi methods
description: High-priority follow-up in the manual replication slice. The public ReplicationApi trait methods are the main application-facing runtime boundary and need explicit docs covering what each method does, what state it reads or mutates, ordering/consistency expectations, error behaviour, and lifecycle constraints for returned providers.

title: T13 Exercise manual checklist concurrency scenarios
description: Use the replicated checklist example to exercise the manual slice scenarios: concurrent adds from two machines; one peer marks an item done while another renames it; one peer deletes an item while another edits it; one peer restarts and retains durable local state; and manual route configuration remains visible and testable without pretending discovery exists.

title: T12 Define the checklist CLI command surface
description:
Define and implement the minimal line-oriented replicated checklist CLI command surface for the manual replication slice. The CLI should be easy to run over SSH and script where practical, while keeping synchronisation explicitly manual.

Startup:

- Start from config only, e.g. replicated-checklist --config alice.toml.
- Do not pass --member separately. The config is node-specific and provides the local member identity.
- Config supplies local member identity, store path, local endpoint bind address, static group id, static ordered members, and static peer routes.
- On startup, install/load exactly one configured static group. If the durable store already has the group, it should match the configured members. If not, create the static local group record.

Group/member commands:

- No group creation, join, leave, or membership modification commands in this slice.
- members: print group id and ordered members.
- me: print local member identity, local endpoint/config summary, store path, and configured routes.

Item addressing:

- list displays both a current list index and the full UUID row id.
- Commands accept either the current list index or the full UUID.
- Indices resolve against the current materialised list at command time. This is good enough for the manual demo even though list order can change after mutations or sync.

Item commands:

- add <text>
- rename <item> <text>
- edit note <item>
- tag add <item> <tag>
- tag rm <item> <tag>
- claim <item>
- complete <item>
- priority <item> <0-255>
- delete <item>
- list
- show <item>
- events [limit]
- sync

Status semantics:

- status is monotonic open -> in_progress -> done.
- claim advances an item to in_progress.
- complete advances an item to done.
- There is no undo command. A mistaken completion is represented by creating a replacement item with a new row id.

Note editing:

- edit note <item> edits a single-line note through a line-editor style prompt prefilled with the current note. Multi-line notes are out of scope for this slice.

edit_count semantics:

- Increment edit_count implicitly whenever any CLI command changes a row value, including add, rename, edit note, tag add, tag rm, claim, complete, and priority.
- delete does not increment because the row disappears.

Events:

- No watch mode.
- events prints process-local event history with timestamps.
- events <limit> prints only the newest limit events.

Synchronisation:

- sync is explicit/manual. Local mutation commands update local durable state but do not intentionally push replication traffic until sync is invoked, so multi-machine scenarios can be stepped through deliberately.

title: T11 Define the replicated checklist row model
description:
Define the replicated checklist row model for the manual replication CLI example. Each checklist item is one replicated row keyed by a generated UUID. The CLI should display the full UUID and may use it directly in commands; this example does not need pretty ids.

Agreed schema proposal:

- text: LINEAR_STRING, default empty string. Main item label; concurrent text edits merge structurally.
- note: LINEAR_STRING, default empty string. Longer free-form notes; useful for concurrent text-edit scenarios.
- tags: LINEAR_LIST<STRING>, default empty list. Exercises list CRDT behaviour for concurrent tag insertions/deletions.
- status: TOTAL_ORDER_FINITE_STATE_REGISTER over open < in_progress < done, default open. Status is monotonic; un-done is represented by creating a copy/replacement item with a new row id.
- priority: LATEST_VALUE_WINS U8 NOT NULL, default 0. Freely editable priority where concurrent edits resolve by the register's LWW total order.
- edit_count: MONOTONIC_COUNTER, default 0. Increment implicitly whenever any CLI command changes a row value, including row creation. Row deletion does not need to increment because the row disappears.

This intentionally skips plain TOTAL_ORDER_REGISTER because status and edit_count already cover the more interesting ordered/monotonic behaviours without adding a redundant checklist concept.

Scenarios to flesh out with concrete CLI commands once the command surface exists:

- Concurrent text edits on the same item should exercise LINEAR_STRING merging.
- Concurrent note edits on the same item should exercise longer LINEAR_STRING merging.
- Concurrent tag insertions/deletions should exercise LINEAR_LIST<STRING> merging.
- Concurrent status transitions should converge to the furthest monotonic state.
- Concurrent priority edits should exercise LATEST_VALUE_WINS conflict resolution.
- Concurrent value-changing edits should accumulate edit_count increments.

title: T10 Add manual synchronisation control to the checklist example
description: Make the checklist example stage local edits without automatically synchronising every change immediately. Add an explicit manual command, such as sync, that triggers outbound replication so concurrent scenarios can be prepared on separate machines before the user presses Enter.

title: T09 Build the replicated checklist CLI example
description:
Add a very small replicated checklist CLI example that can run on two machines on a home network. It is configured entirely from one TOML config file passed as the only CLI argument. The config supplies local member identity, store path, fixed group id, ordered members, local endpoint bind address, and static peer routes. The app installs or loads exactly one configured static group, publishes checklist changes only on explicit sync, and shows replicated events clearly from a line-oriented terminal interface.

Agreed implementation plan:

G01. Add replicated_checklist as a real binary with exactly one positional argument: the config file path. No --member, no separate group id arg, no runtime flags.

G02. Define checklist app config inside that same TOML file, alongside the existing runtime keys. Required fields: local member identity, store path, fixed group id, ordered members. Runtime reachability stays under flotsync.replication.runtime.local-endpoint-bind-addr and [[flotsync.replication.runtime.static-peer-routes]].

G03. Parse and validate config before startup: group id must parse, local member must be present in ordered members, ordered members must be valid and duplicate-free, and static peer routes remain the existing UDP-only runtime config path.

G04. Add a second disk-backed SQLite constructor. Unsynced checklist edits stay process-local in the working copy as agreed; the store path is for runtime/group/update state that has actually been accepted durably.

G05. Install/load exactly one configured static group before loading the runtime. If the store already has the group, verify the configured group id, ordered members, and local member index match. If absent, insert the configured ReplicationGroupRecord with the configured group id and initial version vector. This can be removed later once mutable group support exists.

G06. Start the replication runtime from the configured store plus the same runtime config TOML. The app does not call create_group; it relies on the configured static group and startup store preparation.

G07. Make listener ingestion concurrency-safe with a listener-to-REPL queue, not by sharing ChecklistWorkingCopy across threads. The listener drains ReplicationEvent::DataChanged providers and sends row batches/events into a thread-safe channel. The REPL thread remains the only owner of ChecklistWorkingCopy.

G08. Keep inbound events buffered until sync. On sync: prepare dirty rows from the working copy, call publish_changes if dirty, clear dirty flags only after success, then drain the listener queue into the working copy and apply all queued events. This preserves the publish first, then drain incoming including our own publish result model.

G09. Wire the existing command surface only: item commands, list, show, events, sync, members, me, help, quit. members and me render config/runtime summary; they do not mutate group state.

G10. Keep tests light: config parsing/validation, static group preparation mismatch handling, and sync orchestration around the listener queue. The binary itself is the manual testing tool, so no heavy end-to-end harness.
close_reason: Replicated checklist binary is ready for manual testing

title: T03 Configure manual runtime peer endpoints
description:
Expose runtime-host configuration for manual peer endpoints through TOML/Kompact configuration, without adding a temporary public ReplicationApi route-management surface.

Agreed design:

S01. Replace the temporary runtime discovery source with PreconfiguredPeerRoutesComponent.

S02. PreconfiguredPeerRoutesComponent implements Provide<RouteDiscoveryPort<TransportRouteKey>) and publishes only configured peer routes. No automatic discovery behaviour.

S03. Configuration lives under flotsync.replication.runtime.static-peer-routes as TOML arrays of tables. Each peer route has name, protocol, ip, and port fields. The local bind remains flotsync.replication.runtime.local-endpoint-bind-addr.

S04. Supported protocol for this slice is udp only. Unknown protocols are invalid configuration.

S05. Add a reusable parser/pre-check around Kompact's parsed TOML config so bad static routes fail runtime startup before component execution.

S06. Extend the runtime-system builder path with an in-memory TOML string parameter that is merged into KompactConfig before the system is built. Applications can own file loading themselves.

S07. The route component listens to UdpPort indications and publishes configured peer routes after observing the local endpoint bind announcement from the bridge. This keeps route announcements sequenced after the runtime has a receive socket, without involving LocalEndpointManager explicitly.

S08. Static peer routes require a fixed local endpoint port in this slice. If static routes are configured with local-endpoint-bind-addr port 0, startup should fail.

S09. Tests may use constructor/test-only controls to make route publication timing deterministic, while production uses publish-on-local-endpoint-bound.

S10. Keep relay routes, TCP routes, route refresh loops, route health policy, and automatic discovery out of scope.
close_reason: Completed static peer route configuration for manual replication slice

title: T02 Track authenticated and encrypted replication envelope follow-up
description: Track the immediate post-slice security follow-up: introduce the real authenticated and encrypted replication envelope and payload handling after the manual slice is complete. This slice deliberately does not implement encryption or signing, but the follow-up must remain visible as the next protocol hardening step.

title: T01 Define the manual replication slice contract
description:
Manual functional replication slice contract.

C01. The manual slice supports fixed-membership replication groups between live peers with direct UDP routes configured out of band.

C02. Group size is not fixed by the slice. Any group size supported by the current fixed-membership runtime should be valid when every member is manually configured and live.

C03. Peer reachability is configured through Kompact/runtime configuration, including local bind endpoint and manually supplied peer endpoints. Do not add a temporary public ReplicationApi route-management surface.

C04. Automatic discovery is out of scope. mDNS, peer tracking, reachability probing, relay routes, shared routes, and TCP routes remain later work.

C05. Security is out of scope for this slice. No real encryption or signing; post-slice authenticated/encrypted envelopes are tracked by flotsync-sec.

C06. Group creation covers only fixed initial membership. No change_group_membership, migration, invitations, schema epoch changes, or group close flow.

C07. Replication covers row-level checklist-style changes over the existing schema-operation mapping: insert/update/delete where delete semantics are safe once flotsync-a3v is handled.

C08. Local publish semantics remain: durable local acceptance plus outbound submission before PublishReceipt returns.

C09. Runtime robustness must be strong enough that expected delivery and ordering faults are handled explicitly rather than turning into terminal runtime state. The implementation work for removing the runtime component's set_terminal-style mechanics is owned by flotsync-4x8 and blocks moving beyond this contract.

C10. Delivery robustness required for this slice includes ReliableDelivery sender-side recipient-ack timeout handling and non-terminal handling of expected transient inbound ordering races.

C11. The example app must allow manual synchronisation, so local edits can be staged on multiple peers and flushed with an explicit sync command.

C12. Offline catch-up, Summary/NeedRange/Snapshot/Ack flows, replay after extended disconnection, and relay/mailbox repair are out of scope.

C13. Functional acceptance means manually configured machines can create or join an arbitrary-size fixed group, make checklist edits, manually sync, observe remote events, and preserve durable local state across restart.

C14. Manual test scenarios include concurrent adds, done-vs-rename, delete-vs-edit, restart persistence, and visible static peer configuration.
close_reason: Contract agreed and recorded in the bead

title: Complete the manual functional replication slice
description:
Aligned scope for the manual functional replication slice, explicitly before automatic discovery.

Security follow-up moved out of this parent: authenticated and encrypted replication envelopes are tracked separately in flotsync-sec and are blocked by completion of this slice.

Current scope decision: live UpdateBatch broadcasts in this slice are best-effort. Durable GroupBroadcast work only belongs with the future relay or mailbox path, so broadcast delivery-state persistence is deferred out of this slice.

S01. Manual-slice contract: fixed-membership replication groups of arbitrary size, live peers, direct UDP routes configured out-of-band, no automatic discovery, no migration, and no relay or offline catch-up.

S02. Manual reachability via configuration is complete through TOML/Kompact runtime configuration and static UDP peer routes.

S03. Reliable bootstrap robustness is complete via flotsync-46r. ReliableDelivery now has sender-side recipient-ack timeout retry for bootstrap work that would otherwise remain stuck awaiting semantic acknowledgement.

S04. Runtime delivery robustness is complete via flotsync-4x8. Expected protocol-boundary noise, including live UpdateBatch delivery before local group installation, is logged and dropped instead of poisoning the runtime.

S05. Update mapping for the current row/schema operation path is complete via flotsync-94d. This covers UpdateId derivation, read_versions rules, group binding, compatibility checks, and inbound validation needed by the manual slice.

S06. Remaining correctness work: flotsync-a3v must separate replication tombstones from row garbage collection because delete-vs-edit is part of the checklist scenarios.

S07. Runnable checklist example, manual synchronisation control, checklist row model, and CLI command surface are complete through the flotsync-5j0 child tasks for the example.

S08. Remaining acceptance work: flotsync-5j0.9 must exercise the manual checklist scenarios after delete semantics are safe enough for the delete-vs-edit case.

S09. Deferred protocol and delivery work: durable GroupBroadcast relay state, common outer envelopes, full Summary/NeedRange/Snapshot/Ack catch-up, migration, rich group lifecycle modelling, and security hardening are future work outside this slice.

title: Replace InMemoryData schema Cow with SchemaSource
description: flotsync_data_types::schema::datamodel::InMemoryData currently stores its schema as Cow<'static, Schema>, while SchemaSource lives in flotsync_replication::api. This split is causing unnecessary schema cloning at call sites that need to cross between the two representations, for example where SchemaSource gets turned back into an owned Schema before building InMemoryData. Create a dedicated change to move SchemaSource into the datamodel area and switch InMemoryData to store SchemaSource directly instead of Cow<'static, Schema>. The work should cover constructors and snapshot/decode helpers, update affected call sites, and preserve the existing static-versus-shared ownership behaviour while avoiding avoidable schema clones.
close_reason: Completed; SchemaSource moved into datamodel and InMemoryData now stores SchemaSource directly while preserving static/shared schema ownership.

title: Separate replication tombstones from row garbage collection
description:
Current manual-slice blocker for delete semantics.

Current row-level delete handling physically removes rows from storage. Replication correctness needs durable tombstones to preserve delete causality independently from later row garbage collection.

Required for flotsync-5j0: the checklist delete-vs-edit scenario must not lose the delete intent or allow a concurrent edit to resurrect a deleted row incorrectly. Split durable replication tombstone semantics from eventual row cleanup in the replication store and runtime apply path. Later garbage collection remains a separate follow-up.

Concrete implementation plan:

P01. Model durable row state explicitly. Replace the current Option<RowSnapshot> storage view with a tombstone-aware row state so None means absent and Some(tombstoned=true) means deleted but causally retained.

P02. Stop treating replication deletes as physical storage deletes. Runtime delete application should persist the post-delete row snapshot plus a tombstone flag. Physical removal is future garbage collection work.

P03. Extend SQLite dataset_rows with a tombstone flag that defaults to false for existing rows, and make load/apply round-trip that flag.

P04. Add a small flotsync_data_types API for rebuilding InMemoryData rows as tombstoned from snapshots, plus a helper to query whether an existing row is tombstoned.

P05. Change local delete handling to persist a tombstoned row while still emitting RowChange::Delete.

P06. Change inbound apply visibility semantics: active update emits Upsert, active delete emits Delete, tombstoned-row update persists silently, and repeated tombstoned delete is idempotent and silent.

P07. Allow applied inbound operations to carry no listener-visible RowChange while still producing durable row patches.

P08. Add focused tests for SQLite tombstone round-trip, runtime restart preserving tombstones, inbound update-after-delete not resurrecting a row, and repeated/concurrent delete idempotence.

P09. Keep the checklist app unchanged unless the focused tests show a small adapter fix is required.
notes:
2026-04-30 implementation update: keep tombstone metadata out of bare RowSnapshot, introduce a documented RowRecord for retained row images, replace StoredDatasetRow with replication aliases around RowRecord, rename DatasetRowWrite cases to UpsertActive/UpsertTombstone, allow missing -> tombstone while rejecting tombstone -> active in the store layer, remove the unused physical delete/GC design, remove the SQLite migration helper because no durable databases exist yet, and add focused tests for tombstone storage semantics.
2026-04-30 implementation result: introduced RowRecord and replication row aliases, replaced DatasetRowWrite::Put/Delete with UpsertActive/UpsertTombstone, added SQLite transition validation that allows missing -> tombstone and rejects tombstone -> active, removed the SQLite tombstone migration helper, and added focused store/runtime/datamodel coverage. Created follow-up flotsync-uvo for the public ReplicationStore conformance suite. Local validation passed: cargo fmt --all; cargo test -p flotsync_data_types -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -p flotsync_replication -- --test-threads=1; cargo check --workspace --all-targets; git diff --check.

title: Reduce replication runtime dataset loading to touched rows
description: The runtime currently materialises whole datasets into memory during transactional publish/apply flows. This was not the intended design and causes both unnecessary memory use and extra copying. Follow up on flotsync-73q.4 by changing the storage/runtime boundary so transactional work can load and write back only the rows actually needed for the current operation, instead of full dataset images.
notes:
Implementation plan for flotsync-i5b:

1. Replace the snapshot-shaped runtime store API with row-granular transaction methods:
- DatasetRowSlice { group_id, dataset_id, dataset_exists, rows: Vec<(RowKey, Option<RowSnapshot>)> }
- DatasetRowPatch { group_id, dataset_id, actions: Vec<DatasetRowWrite> }
- DatasetRowWrite::{Put, Delete}
- load_dataset_rows(group_id, dataset_id, row_keys)
- apply_dataset_row_patch(patch)

2. Remove load_dataset_snapshot/apply_dataset_snapshot from the hot runtime path for now.

3. Keep schemas keyed by DatasetId for this task. dataset_exists stays explicit on the read side so the runtime can distinguish an empty existing dataset from a first-touch dataset.

4. Refactor the runtime helper layer so publish/apply only hold highly ephemeral in-memory slices for the touched rows. No resident full dataset cache and no full before/after diffing.

5. Local publish and inbound apply both load only touched row keys, mutate those partial in-memory slices, persist explicit Put/Delete row actions, and commit together with update-log/VV changes.

6. Add store/runtime tests for partial row load-write, untouched-row preservation, and rollback of row-granular writes.

title: Move replication runtime tests onto the in-memory sqlite store
description: Replace the current StoreStub-based runtime tests with the in-memory rusqlite ReplicationStore backend. Add fixtures for static-schema and Arc-schema sources, and cover restart recovery, rollback on failed transactions, durable PublishReceipt semantics, persisted dataset_updates, and causal buffering across reloads.
acceptance_criteria: The replication runtime test suite exercises the new store-backed path end-to-end, including restart and rollback cases, and no longer depends on the old non-persistent store stub for primary coverage.

title: Persist dataset_updates and apply causally ready chains transactionally
description: Replace the in-memory pending update queue with persisted dataset_updates records for inbound and outbound replication traffic. Support efficient lookup of not-yet-applied candidates, load those candidates into memory when checking causal readiness, and commit an entire newly-ready apply chain atomically so failures do not leave partially advanced replication state behind.
acceptance_criteria: Inbound and outbound updates are durably recorded, duplicate and conflict detection use persisted records, and applying one update together with any newly-ready successors is all-or-nothing at the transaction boundary.

title: Hydrate replication_groups and membership snapshots from ReplicationStore
description: Refactor runtime startup so persisted replication group metadata and canonical member snapshots are loaded from ReplicationStore, then used to seed SharedGroupMemberships. Remove LocalRuntimeState as the whole-runtime source of truth and keep only derived read models plus transaction-scoped metadata in the component.
acceptance_criteria: Runtime startup can recover persisted replication groups after restart, SharedGroupMemberships is derived from store state, and the component no longer treats its own in-memory map as the authoritative group registry.

title: Implement an in-memory sqlx ReplicationStore backend for the replication slice
description: Add a sqlx-backed ReplicationStore implementation that runs entirely in named shared-cache SQLite memory mode so tests stay isolated and concurrent-safe. Share an Arc<SqlitePool> across the store, acquire a dedicated connection per transaction, configure the pool so the in-memory database stays alive, and persist the replication slice using immutable group membership, a version-vector blob, row-wise dataset persistence driven by the aligned storage action API, and immutable UpdateBatch blobs with separately tracked applied state.
acceptance_criteria: The backend opens without creating on-disk files, initialises its schema automatically, keeps the named in-memory database alive through the pool, begins write transactions with BEGIN IMMEDIATE, preserves full u64 update ordering, enforces the agreed group and update immutability rules, reuses shared codec helpers instead of duplicating encode/decode logic, and is usable as the primary test store for runtime integration tests.
notes:
Backend plan aligned with review:
107. Use sqlx SQLite in named shared in-memory mode, with an Arc<SqlitePool> shared by the store and a dedicated acquired connection per transaction.
108. Configure the pool so the in-memory database stays alive, then begin each write transaction with BEGIN IMMEDIATE.
109. Keep substantial SQL as static strings and rely on sqlx's per-connection statement cache; if hot statements need warming, do that in after_connect rather than pretending a global sqlx::query value carries prepared-statement state.
110. Store group membership as immutable rows plus a stored member_count, and allow only the version_vector blob to change on an existing group.
111. Store each UpdateBatch as one immutable blob; only the applied flag may change after insertion.
112. Preserve full u64 update ordering by storing versions in fixed-width big-endian blobs, with explicit comments explaining why SQLite's lexicographic BLOB ordering is correct here.
113. Replace whole-dataset snapshot blobs with row-wise persistence driven by the explicit storage action API.

title: Define transactional ReplicationStore boundary for the first replication slice
description: Expand ReplicationStore from identity/schema lookup into an object-safe, async-safe transaction boundary for the first replication slice. Cover replication group metadata, canonical members and member indices, version vectors, dataset snapshot access, persisted dataset update records, and schema lookup that can return either shared Arc-backed schemas or static schemas. Keep the scope deliberately to the current replication runtime needs rather than the full long-term storage abstraction.
acceptance_criteria: A concrete trait and DTO shape exists for first-slice replication state, transaction semantics are explicit about read-your-own-writes plus commit/rollback, and the schema source can represent Arc or &'static schemas without forcing extra cloning.

title: Remove terminal runtime fault handling for expected inbound delivery errors
description:
The manual replication slice must not record terminal runtime state for expected delivery and ordering faults. The current runtime component uses set_terminal-style mechanics and fails subsequent API calls after inbound group-delivery errors. That was acceptable as a conservative first-slice guard, but it is too strong for the manual functional slice.

Agreed handling policy:

R01. Remove the runtime component's terminal_fault / set_terminal-style mechanics entirely, including RuntimeTerminated API failure behaviour.

R02. API calls should fail only when the component is actually unavailable, using the normal ask failure path.

R03. Production protocol-boundary noise should log and drop: unknown group updates, wrong runtime message kind on a delivery channel, and corrupt runtime payloads.

R04. Test-only protocol-boundary noise may panic or otherwise fail loudly instead of growing elaborate result plumbing.

R05. Unknown group updates are treated as not interested in this group. Drop with logging; do not buffer pre-bootstrap races.

R06. Reliable-delivery messages that cannot be decoded or accepted must not complete the processed promise. Ack/retry behaviour remains with flotsync-46r.

R07. Store access failures from inbound delivery are fatal for now. A closed or corrupted database should not leave a ghost component that only logs repeated failures.

R08. Listener errors are treated as permanent boundary failures. The runtime does not own listener retry and may terminate/fault on listener failure.

R09. Conflicting duplicate payloads should log loudly, not apply, not advance state, and not kill the runtime.

R10. No custom Kompact recovery is required in this task. Stop self-poisoning expected errors; let real panics/component faults flow through normal Kompact supervision.

R11. Keep tests minimal: prove an unknown-group inbound update does not poison later API calls, and keep existing direct error tests where useful. Avoid an exhaustive corrupt-payload matrix.

title: Add sender-side recipient-ack timeout retry to ReliableDelivery
description:
ReliableDelivery currently leaves sender work items stuck in AwaitingRecipientAck when a recipient never completes semantic processing or rejects an inbound message before acking. Add a sender-side timeout that re-enters the existing retry path when no recipient ack is observed, and update runtime/bootstrap handling to surface failures with context. Discovered while reviewing the first replication slice runtime on flotsync-75s.

Agreed implementation plan:

P01. Add a recipient-ack timeout config key flotsync.reliable-delivery.recipient-ack-timeout with a five-minute production default. Tests may override it to a short duration.

P02. After an outbound reliable envelope transport send succeeds, move the sender work item to AwaitingRecipientAck and schedule its sender retry key for recipient-ack-timeout.

P03. When that retry key fires while the work item is still AwaitingRecipientAck and RecipientAckStatus is still Pending, log the timeout, move the item back to PendingRoute, and reuse the normal direct dispatch path. The retry sends the same message_id and payload, not a new logical reliable message.

P04. Recipient acks may arrive late while the sender work item is PendingRoute or AttemptingDirect after a timeout-driven retry. Accept them for any existing sender work item, cancel the sender retry key, and mark RecipientAckStatus::Observed.

P05. Make receiver duplicate handling state-aware. If a duplicate inbound envelope arrives while AwaitingProcessed, drop it because the receiver already has the envelope locally. If it arrives while AckPending, retry/schedule the cached recipient ack. If it arrives while AckInFlight, drop it because an ack send is already in progress. If the receiver already sent the ack and removed inbound state, a later resend is processed as fresh; bootstrap installation is idempotent for the same group definition and will ack again.

P06. Update the runtime bootstrap processed-promise comment to document the contract: processed is completed only after durable bootstrap install; failures intentionally withhold processed so the sender-side recipient-ack timeout drives redelivery.

P07. Add tests for timeout redelivery, late ack acceptance after a timeout retry, and receiver duplicate handling. Verify with focused ReliableDelivery tests, the full flotsync_replication test suite, and clippy.

title: Investigate flaky Linux workspace test failures in pr-linux CI
description: PR #2 hit a flaky failure in the pr-linux GitHub Actions workflow on April 12, 2026 (run 24302768196, job 70958911092). The failing tests were driver::udp::tests::udp_connected_send_and_receive_work, driver::udp::tests::udp_unconnected_bind_send_and_receive_work, kompact::tests::tcp_listener_reuse_config_allows_binding_to_a_reserved_port, and kompact::tests::udp_bridge_broadcasts_socket_activity_but_send_results_stay_private. The failure did not reproduce locally with cargo test -p flotsync_io or cargo test --workspace on macOS, so this likely needs investigation on Linux or in a Linux-like environment. Capture a deterministic reproduction path if possible, then stabilise or redesign the flaky socket/runtime test expectations.

title: Standardise async test support with eventually-style waits and shared harnesses
description: Create one shared test-support layer for flotsync_io, flotsync_udpour, and flotsync_replication that replaces the current ad-hoc polling loops and fragmented harness code. Scope includes an eventually-style helper for predicate-based waiting, shared buffered probe utilities for mpsc-backed event streams, common Kompact lifecycle helpers, and reusable transport/harness fixtures so crates stop re-implementing near-identical test infrastructure.
acceptance_criteria: There is one sanctioned eventually-style wait helper used across the targeted crates; duplicated buffered receiver and polling helpers are removed or folded into shared support; new or migrated tests use the shared harness abstractions rather than crate-local bespoke fixtures.

title: Serialise socket-heavy test harnesses across crates
description: The current replication and IO integration tests use real local UDP sockets and rely on ad-hoc serialisation. Evaluate project-level test serialisation and/or a shared cross-process lock so socket-heavy tests do not interfere across crates or test binaries. This was discovered while implementing flotsync-w6r.

title: Fix egress adopt_payload to accept payloads already owned by the same pool
description: EgressPool::adopt_payload currently rejects pooled IoPayload fragments once they have been cloned, even when those fragments are already owned by the same egress pool. UDPour currently has to fall back to copying in this case. Adjust the adopt logic so payloads already retargeted to the destination pool are accepted instead of treated as shared ownership failures.

title: Add mockable clocks for UDPour timing tests
description: Replace real sleeps in UDPour timing tests with mockable or controllable clocks/timers so tombstone expiry, pacing, and retry behavior can be tested deterministically without wall-clock waits.
design:
Goal
- Make Kompact runtimes testable against a manually advanced timer without changing the default production behaviour.
- Preserve current scheduling semantics for one-shot, periodic, cancellation, and zero-delay timers.

Chosen Route
- Keep the existing Kompact config seam at `KompactConfig::timer(...)`.
- Preserve Kompact's concrete `timer::TimerRef` path by teaching `hierarchical_hash_wheel_timer` to expose one queue-based timer reference that can be driven either by a dedicated thread or by a manual driver.
- Add a test-oriented manual timer component in Kompact that wraps the manual driver and exposes a separate control handle to the harness.

P1. Timer Crate Refactor
- Extract the queue message type, timer-reference construction, and schedule/cancel plumbing out of `thread_timer.rs` into a shared queue-driven core.
- Keep `thread_timer::TimerRef` as the public scheduling handle so downstream API churn stays minimal.
- Refactor `TimerWithThread` to delegate to that shared core plus a thread loop.
- Add a new manual driver type, tentatively `ManualTimer` or `DrivenTimer`, with `timer_ref()`, `advance_by`, `advance_one`, `advance_until_idle`, `current_time`, and one lightweight observability method such as `is_idle` or `next_due_in`.
- Ensure queued schedule/cancel messages are drained both before and during advancement so commands issued between advances, or triggered indirectly during execution, are observed deterministically.
- Define zero-delay semantics explicitly and match the threaded behaviour as closely as possible.

P2. Timer Crate Validation
- Add parity tests that run equivalent scripted schedule/cancel sequences against both the threaded and manual drivers.
- Cover one-shot timers, periodic timers, cancellation before fire, cancellation after enqueue, zero-delay timers, large skips, multiple timers expiring on the same tick, and schedule-from-trigger behaviour.
- Add explicit tests for `advance_until_idle` so pathological periodic workloads fail loudly instead of spinning silently.
- Document any deliberate semantic difference that cannot be eliminated.

P3. Kompact Integration
- Add a `ManualTimerComponent` that stores shared manual-timer state and implements `TimerComponent` by returning the shared queue-based `TimerRef`.
- Add a companion `ManualTimerControl` handle for tests. Harnesses should keep this handle outside the runtime and use it to advance time.
- Keep the production default unchanged; tests opt in by installing the manual timer through `KompactConfig::timer(...)`.
- If useful upstream, add a small helper constructor that returns the builder closure together with the retained control handle.
- Avoid widening Kompact abstractions unless the shared-reference route proves insufficient.
- Fallback if necessary: generalise `TimerRefFactory` and `TimerManager` away from the concrete `timer::TimerRef` alias as a second-stage patch.

P4. Kompact Validation
- Add system-level tests showing that scheduled timeouts do not fire until manual time advances past the deadline.
- Cover periodic rescheduling, cancellation, dropping components with pending timers, and runtime shutdown with queued timeouts.
- Add a multi-component test to verify deterministic delivery when several components share the same manual timer.
- Document the contract between time advancement and component execution, because advancing time will enqueue work but may still require the harness to wait for component turns to run.

P5. Flotsync Adoption
- Extend the local Kompact test builders so they can install the manual timer and return its control handle.
- Migrate the timing-sensitive UDPour runtime tests in this issue first: pacing, retries, retention or tombstone expiry, and similar wall-clock-driven cases.
- Use the shared eventually-style helper from `flotsync-6uy` for post-advance observation. The manual timer removes real sleeps; it does not remove the need to wait for asynchronous side effects to become visible.
- Keep a smaller set of real-time smoke tests where the purpose is explicitly to cover threaded scheduling and integration with the live runtime.

P6. Risks And Upstreaming Notes
- The existing `simulation::SimulationTimer` is not a direct fit for Kompact because it is not queue-reference based and relies on `Rc`, so it should inform semantics rather than be wrapped directly.
- The most delicate semantic area is commands racing with `advance_by`; those rules need to be explicit and tested.
- Prefer an upstreamable manual-driver abstraction in the timer crate over a flotsync-only test shim.

Done Means
- A Kompact runtime can be built with a manual timer and externally advanced deterministically.
- The UDPour timing tests covered by `flotsync-jgg` stop depending on wall-clock sleeps for timer progression.
- The default threaded timer remains the production default and retains its current behaviour.

title: Prevent duplicate UDPour delivery from late retransmissions
description: Add receiver-side delivered tombstones and a payload retransmit flag so late shared-route repair traffic does not cause duplicate delivery. Cover this with a receiver unit regression and a runtime/shared-route smoke test where one receiver repairs while another already delivered.

title: Implement datagram multipart transfer in route transport
description: Implement the datagram multipart protocol defined in docs/datagram_multipart_transfer.md inside the route-transport layer. Cover frame encoding/decoding, sender retention and id reuse policy, receiver reassembly and timeout handling, NeedParts bitmap handling, NoLongerAvailable responses, shared-route retransmission behavior, and tests for confusion recovery and checksum validation.

title: Design logical-payload transfer over datagram routes
description: Define how the lower route-transport layer handles logical payloads that do not fit into a single datagram. Cover fragmentation/framing, reassembly, missing-piece requests, spill-to-disk buffering, retransmission expectations, and how this integrates with route-local Ack/Nack semantics without leaking multipart details into GroupBroadcast or ReliableDelivery. This task must first survey relevant existing protocols and framing schemes before proposing a custom design.

title: Migrate protobuf runtime/codegen from protobuf v3 to buffa
description: The current protobuf crate in use is end-of-life, so migrate now while the codebase is still pre-production. Key points from the assessment: the .proto schemas themselves look compatible, but the handwritten transport layer is tightly coupled to rust-protobuf v3 APIs (MessageField, EnumOrUnknown, set_* oneof helpers, parse_from_bytes/write_to_bytes, generated ::new()). The migration surface is concentrated in flotsync_messages plus a small discovery follow-up. The current build uses pure Rust protobuf_codegen::Codegen::pure(); buffa instead requires either buffa-build with protoc, buffa-build with .use_buf(), or external buf generate, so CI/dev tooling must be updated accordingly. First implementation spike should confirm generated module paths for package-less protos, preserve current public module names if needed, and start with a narrow config (no JSON, no views, no use_bytes_type) before evaluating follow-up optimizations.

title: Design single-recipient durable delivery and relay mailbox protocol
description: Define a top-level protocol abstraction for durable delivery to one specific recipient outside normal GroupBroadcast fan-out. This is needed for bootstrap flows such as new-group establishment before a shared group key exists. Specify the relay-assisted mailbox mechanism needed to store and forward recipient-addressed encrypted payloads, how sender/recipient identity and retrieval work, how this interacts with later GroupBroadcast/Replication flows, and what delivery and persistence guarantees are required.
close_reason: Reviewed and completed via commit ff24597 (single-recipient durable delivery and relay mailbox protocol spec).

title: Define GroupBroadcast domain types and internal contracts
description: Introduce the code-level domain model needed for GroupBroadcast without building the full runtime yet. Define the envelope, delivery class, message reference, per-target queue state/outcome types, relay persistence confirmation shape, optional delivery receipt shape, and internal runtime-facing contracts needed between replication, broadcast, discovery, and persistence layers.
close_reason: Reviewed and completed via commit 128556f (delivery domain contracts and module scaffolding).

title: Map GroupBroadcast semantics onto flotsync_io Kompact transports
description: Define how GroupBroadcast delivery maps onto the current flotsync_io Kompact APIs. Specify when UDP versus TCP should be used, how transport-level send outcomes relate to semantic delivery, how relay persistence confirmations fit in, and which transport/backpressure signals the broadcast runtime must interpret. This task depends on the queue/state-machine specification.

title: Specify GroupBroadcast queue state machine and ownership model
description: Define the concrete GroupBroadcast state machine for per-message/per-target fan-out. Specify active states, terminal outcomes, legal transitions, retry triggers, ownership boundaries between discovery, broadcast, replication, and persistence, and which facts are internal bookkeeping versus externally observable. This task should produce an implementation-oriented spec that later runtime work can follow.
close_reason: Reviewed and completed via commit 34438dc (GroupBroadcast route queue semantics spec).

title: Prepare Replication runtime boundary for GroupBroadcast integration
description: After the queue/state model, transport mapping, and domain types are settled, extend the replication runtime boundary so a future implementation can wire GroupBroadcast in cleanly. Specify or add the internal hooks for enqueueing messages, receiving reachability updates, handling relay durability outcomes, and bridging replication events into GroupBroadcast without prematurely exposing transport details in the public Replication API.
design:
Agreed implementation scope for codex/flotsync-75s-replication-slice:

1. Make load_replication_runtime(...) return a concrete runtime behind the existing public ReplicationApi boundary.
2. Extend ReplicationStore minimally so the runtime can obtain the local member identity and dataset schemas it needs for this slice; do not attempt the full storage abstraction from flotsync-73q here.
3. Introduce a runtime-owned ordered hosted-group model alongside SharedGroupMemberships. SharedGroupMemberships/TrieSet remain the fast membership-presence view for delivery, but canonical member order must not be inferred from Trie iteration because TrieMap/TrieSet are backed by AHashMap and do not provide a stable order.
4. Build an internal runtime host around the existing delivery components so replication can update group membership snapshots, submit outbound work, and consume inbound deliveries.
5. Implement create_group for the fixed-membership, online-only path using ReliableDelivery for bootstrap. Do not support create_group.initial_state in this slice.
6. Materialise per-(group_id, dataset_id) schema-aware InMemoryData state and map public RowMutation values into local schema operations before transport.
7. Add the smallest useful replication wire/messages layer for this slice: one bootstrap message carried by ReliableDelivery and one live update message carried by GroupBroadcast. Do not try to complete the full Replication protocol state machine in this branch.
8. Implement publish_changes as: local apply, outbound GroupBroadcast fan-out, inbound remote apply, then ReplicationEvent::DataChanged emission on the receiving runtime.
9. Add one full-stack loopback test with two runtimes proving create_group followed by publish_changes produces a remote DataChanged event through the public API.
10. Keep the branch deliberately narrow and honest: fixed membership, live peers, direct routes only.

Explicit deferrals for this branch:

11. No change_group_membership implementation and no migration/invitation-policy flow beyond the bootstrap needed for create_group.
12. No Summary/NeedRange/Snapshot/Ack catch-up flow, no offline repair, and no causality-blocked replay beyond what falls out trivially from the narrow live-update path.
13. No relay/mailbox integration, no restart recovery, and no signature-verification enforcement yet.
notes:
Stage 1 implemented on codex/flotsync-75s-replication-slice: added minimal ReplicationStore hooks (local member identity plus dataset schema lookup), introduced runtime-owned HostedGroup/HostedGroups state with canonical member order and member-index lookup, and added unit tests for canonical ordering, duplicate rejection, and delivery snapshot derivation. load_replication_runtime now validates the store boundary by loading the local member identity before returning the current placeholder error.
Correction to stage 1: dropped the separate HostedGroup/HostedGroups model. SharedGroupMemberships remains authoritative, and GroupMemberships now stores GroupMembers (TrieMap-backed membership with fixed canonical member indices as values). GroupBroadcast was adjusted to iterate GroupMembers directly, and the stage-1 tests now cover indexed membership behaviour on the authoritative snapshot.
Stage 2 and the first half of stage 3 are now implemented. load_replication_runtime returns a concrete runtime with a fully wired internal delivery host, one externally bound UDP socket, and a background inbound-delivery loop. Added a minimal protobuf-backed runtime bootstrap message (group id plus canonical member order) and implemented create_group for the narrow first slice: it rejects initial_state, validates fixed membership including the local member, installs the group locally, and sends recipient-addressed reliable bootstrap envelopes to the remote members. Inbound reliable bootstrap messages install the same group membership snapshot remotely and complete the reliable-delivery processed promise so the ack path can run. Added an end-to-end replication test that loads two runtimes, publishes direct peer routes, calls create_group via the public API, and verifies remote membership installation with preserved canonical member indices. publish_changes, dataset state, group-broadcasted updates, and listener DataChanged events remain the next step.
Tightened the first runtime slice after review: GroupMembers is now canonical-order-only via from_ordered_members; the runtime host no longer relies on a fixed sleep for direct route publication and instead waits for route visibility in both semantic owners; ReplicationConfig now carries the external UDP bind address and defaults it to 0.0.0.0:0 so the runtime is no longer loopback-only by default. Added follow-up flotsync-46r for the ReliableDelivery sender-side recipient-ack timeout gap.
Implemented the first publish/apply replication slice on top of the delivery layer. Added a minimal runtime UpdateBatch message carrying one shared UpdateId plus per-dataset schema operations, runtime-owned hosted dataset state, local publish_changes staging/encoding/commit, inbound GroupBroadcast decode/apply, and listener-facing DataChanged emission. Added an end-to-end test that boots two runtimes, creates a group, publishes one row mutation, and verifies remote event delivery and replicated state. Verification: cargo test -p flotsync_replication; cargo clippy -p flotsync_replication --all-targets.
Implemented the review follow-up for the first replication slice: GroupMembers now only builds from canonical ordered members; hosted groups now track a real VersionVector instead of a local counter; UpdateBatch carries ReadVV on the runtime wire; inbound live updates deduplicate by UpdateId, buffer until LocalVV >= ReadVV and the producer version is the next expected one, then drain buffered updates once dependencies are satisfied; inbound delivery faults now terminate the runtime and fail subsequent API calls instead of being swallowed. Added focused tests for causal buffering/duplicate suppression. Follow-up tracking for future durability semantics: flotsync-74h.
2026-04-13 review-driven follow-up worklist agreed for this branch:

A. Architecture
1. Move the bulk of replication runtime logic into a Kompact ReplicationRuntimeComponent.
2. Keep the public ReplicationApi surface as a thin ask or tell bridge into that component.
3. Remove the external delivery loop and related imperative scaffolding once the component owns inbound and outbound delivery handling.

B. Runtime host and local endpoint binding
4. Split DeliveryRuntimeHost construction into separate build, connect, start, and shutdown phases.
5. Introduce readable local type aliases for long delivery port types and route transport types.
6. Replace repeated ad hoc connection error formatting with shared helpers.
7. Replace the UDP activation policy magic string with a named constant or helper for OnFirstUse.
8. Add a Kompact config key for runtime host control timeouts and use it for start, bind, and shutdown waits.
9. Move the local UDP bind concern out of public ReplicationConfig.
10. Introduce a simple internal local endpoint binder stub that owns one configured UDP bind for this slice.
11. Remove RuntimeUdpObserver as part of the binder stub work.
12. Make DeliveryRuntimeHost::drop a last resort guard after an explicit shutdown path exists.
13. Remove production use of flotsync.io.bind-reuse-address in the runtime host.

C. Discovery and temporary test scaffolding

15. Document publish_route_update as temporary until real discovery wiring exists.
16. Clean up publish_direct_peer_route readability with local aliases or helpers.

D. Error and config surface

18. Add a GroupMembers singleton convenience constructor.
19. Add sensible Default impls for SenderConfig, ReceiverConfig, and UDPourConfig, then use UDPourConfig::default() in the runtime host.

E. Wire format and payload handling
20. Add documentation comments to replication.proto that explain message expectations and ordering significance.
21. Rename read_vv to read_versions in proto and Rust.

23. Separate runtime in-memory message to protobuf conversion from protobuf to bytes conversion.

26. Replace stringly decode_version_vector errors with a real internal error type and treat unsupported runtime wire shapes as bugs rather than soft strings.

F. Readability and tests

G. Existing agreed follow-ups to keep referenced

34. Keep flotsync-36j referenced for the UDP observer backlog concern until that code is removed.

- R2 Runtime API ergonomics in runtime/handle.rs: introduce ApiResult/ApiFuture aliases, prefer actor ask conveniences over manual promise boilerplate, simplify or remove resolve_runtime_future if snafu wrapping can express the same behaviour directly, and remove slice wording from public docs.

- R4 Local state backend abstraction in runtime/in_memory.rs: rename Hosted* to Local*, define an internal storage/state trait for the in-memory backend, move repeated group logic onto the group state type, move mutation-scope helpers closer to RowMutation or the storage contract, and push reusable VersionVector helpers to the right abstraction layer.
- R5 Message/wire simplification in runtime/messages.rs: clarify the domain/proto/wire separation, reduce unnecessary parallel DTOs where practical, and flatten large nested constructor transforms into named local fields.
- R6 Component control-flow refactor in runtime/component.rs: extract helpers for publish preflight, inbound apply readiness, listener notification, and reliable bootstrap handling; replace Vec-based de-duplication with HashSet where appropriate; move repeated can-apply and decode-with-member-count logic onto local group state.

- C1 macOS CI failure in flotsync_udpour runtime_tests::repair_path_emits_need_parts_and_retransmits_only_missing_part appears to be a timer-processing race in the test harness, not a reproduced local protocol bug.

title: Support flotsync_discovery on Windows CI
description: Make the discovery crates build and test on native Windows CI. Investigate the Packet.lib/Npcap or WinPcap dependency path from pnet, decide whether to install the required SDK in CI or make discovery networking support Windows-optional, and then restore flotsync_discovery and flotsync_discovery_cli coverage in the Windows job.

title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflows
description: Add GitHub Actions workflows for the Rust workspace. Require Linux CI on pull requests with fmt, clippy, workspace tests, and discovery feature-matrix checks. Add scheduled/manual native test runs for macOS and Windows and compile-only portability checks for mobile targets.

title: Make egress reservations growable or hint-based for send_with-style APIs
description: The new TcpSessionRef::send_with API currently requires callers to provide a hard upper bound for the serialized payload size. Revisit the egress reservation/write_with API so callers can treat the size as an optional hint or allow automatic growth during serialization without manual retry bookkeeping. This should preserve backpressure and fairness semantics while making the high-level send helpers significantly simpler to use correctly.

title: Improve flotsync_io Kompact open ergonomics
description: Make TCP open/listen futures resolve only when usable, remove redundant establishment events from steady-state streams, remove UDP socket reservation from the public Kompact API, switch UDP open requests to create-and-open with auto-assigned SocketId and UdpOpenRequestId correlation, and use UdpLocalBind consistently across UDP bind/connect. Update docs, examples, and tests accordingly.

title: Switch flotsync_io runtime logging to Kompact logger
description: Refactor non-test runtime logging in flotsync_io driver and pool code to use KompactLogger instead of the global log facade, add explicit logger injection to IoDriver, and isolate example-app logging so it no longer interferes with library/runtime logs. Discovered while finishing flotsync_io_examples netcat logging.

title: Add integration test matrix for correctness and flow control
description: Test lease starvation, suspend/resume transitions, UDP multicast/broadcast behavior, and TCP lifecycle edge cases.

title: Implement TCP listener and accept path (listener-owned connections)

close_reason: Completed: implemented TCP listener bind/listen/close support, paused accepted-connection adoption via AdoptAccepted/RejectAccepted, Kompact TcpListener/PendingTcpSession/TcpListenerRef model, and raw plus Kompact test coverage for accept, reject, and drop-to-reject flows.

title: Implement explicit send ack/nack and suspend/resume semantics

title: Implement Kompact IoDriver and IoBridge typed integration

title: Pilot migration: replace Tokio-based UDP service path in discovery

title: RFC: flotsync_io API and backpressure state machines
